question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the combination of unique `method` + `path` + `code` label tuples to create O(n^3) label cardinality in `durationHistogram`, where n is the number of unique values per dimension, causing the OpenTelemetry SDK to allocate gigabytes of memory and OOM-kill the process, preventing transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where `res.end()` is called multiple times (e.g., by triggering an error handler that calls `res.end()` after the route handler already called it), causing `responseSize` to be double-counted in `responseSizeHistogram` and corrupting the response size metrics used to detect abnormal transaction gossip data exfiltration?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `res.write` and `res.end` are monkey-patched without checking if they have already been patched (e.g., by another middleware), causing double-wrapping that leads to `responseSize` being counted twice per chunk, corrupting `responseSizeHistogram` and masking large transaction gossip data leaks?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request that causes a downstream middleware to replace `res.write` or `res.end` after `metricsMiddleware` has already patched them, breaking the `originalWrite`/`originalEnd` chain and causing `responseSize` to never be updated, silently zeroing out all `responseSizeHistogram` records for transaction gossip endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: initMetrics()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger repeated server restarts (via crafted requests that cause unhandled exceptions) to force `initMetrics()` to re-register Prometheus metrics with the same names, causing the OpenTelemetry SDK to throw duplicate instrument registration errors that crash the process and prevent transaction gossip monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: initMetrics()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `nodejs_process_cpu_usage_percentage` observable gauge callback, which captures `process.cpuUsage()` deltas, by sending CPU-intensive requests that inflate the reported CPU percentage above alerting thresholds, triggering auto-scaling or circuit-breaker responses that cut off transaction gossip endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: initMetrics()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests that cause large heap allocations (e.g., requesting large transaction history pages), inflating `nodejs_process_memory_heap_used_bytes` observable gauge values above alerting thresholds, triggering automated pod restarts that interrupt transaction gossip propagation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting in `metricsMiddleware` itself (it only records metrics, does not enforce limits) to send millions of requests per second, saturating `allRequestCounter.add(1)` calls and causing the OpenTelemetry counter to overflow or lock, blocking all metric recording and blinding operators to transaction gossip attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with extremely long `req.url` values (e.g., 1MB query strings) that cause `new URL(req.url, 'http://localhost')` to consume excessive CPU during URL parsing, creating a CPU DoS that delays processing of legitimate transaction gossip requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: toOpenApiPath()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a URL path with thousands of `:param`-style segments (e.g., `/a/:b/:c/:d/...`) that causes the regex `/:([^/]+)/g` in `path.replace()` to perform catastrophic backtracking, consuming 100% CPU and preventing transaction gossip requests from being processed?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: toOpenApiPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request where `res.locals[requestPathLabel]` is set to a very long string by a prior middleware, causing `toOpenApiPath()` to store that string as a Prometheus label, creating a memory leak in the metrics registry that eventually causes OOM and prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: authenticate()] Can an unprivileged external user send thousands of concurrent requests to the metrics endpoint with random credentials, causing `basicAuth(req)` to parse the `Authorization` header on every request and `tsscmp()` to execute constant-time comparisons at high frequency, creating a CPU saturation attack that delays transaction gossip request processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `Date.now()` is used for `startTime` and `duration` calculation, and by sending requests that are kept open for very long durations (hours), cause `durationHistogram.record(duration, labels)` to record values far outside the configured `durationBuckets = [25, 100, 250, 500]`, corrupting histogram overflow buckets and masking slow transaction gossip attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request that triggers a streaming response (multiple `res.write()` calls with large chunks), causing `responseSize` to accumulate to a value exceeding `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, resulting in floating-point precision loss in `responseSizeHistogram.record()` and corrupting response size metrics for transaction gossip endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of a `res.on('close')` handler (only `res.on('finish')` is registered) to cause `inFlightCounter` to never decrement when a client disconnects before the response is finished, permanently inflating the in-flight counter and causing false capacity alerts that prevent operators from accepting new transaction gossip connections?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request that causes an unhandled exception in a downstream middleware after `inFlightCounter.add(1)` but before `res.on('finish')` fires, causing the counter to leak upward indefinitely and eventually triggering automated load-shedding that blocks all transaction gossip queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send HTTP/1.1 requests with `Connection: keep-alive` and never close the connection, keeping `inFlightCounter` permanently elevated for each open connection, and by opening thousands of such connections exhaust the server's connection pool and prevent new transaction gossip requests from being accepted?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `metricsMiddleware` calls `next()` unconditionally for non-metrics paths, meaning there is no request size or rate gate before metric instrumentation begins, allowing the attacker to use the metrics overhead itself as an amplification vector to slow down transaction gossip processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to the metrics endpoint when `authentication` is disabled, read the `api_all_request_in_processing_total` gauge value, and use it to precisely time a burst attack that fills the in-flight capacity exactly when the server is most vulnerable, suppressing transaction gossip during peak load?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user read the unauthenticated metrics endpoint to observe `api_request_duration_milliseconds` histogram data for transaction gossip endpoints, inferring database query patterns and timing windows to craft targeted slow-query attacks that delay gossip propagation?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user access the metrics endpoint (when `authentication` is false) to read `api_all_success` vs `api_all_errors` counters, determining the ratio of successful to failed transaction gossip queries and identifying which endpoints are most vulnerable to targeted DoS?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user access the unauthenticated metrics endpoint to read `nodejs_process_memory_heap_used_bytes` and `nodejs_process_memory_rss_bytes` gauges, determining the exact memory headroom available and crafting a memory exhaustion attack sized precisely to OOM the process and prevent transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user access the unauthenticated metrics endpoint to read `nodejs_process_cpu_usage_percentage` gauge values, timing their CPU-intensive attack requests to coincide with existing high CPU load periods to push the server over its capacity threshold and suppress transaction gossip processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` body and no `Content-Length` header, causing `parseInt(req.headers['content-length'] ?? '0', 10) || 0` to record `0` for `requestSizeHistogram` even for very large request bodies, hiding large transaction gossip suppression payloads from size-based anomaly detection?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/metricsHandler.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: metricsMiddleware()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with multiple `Content-Length` headers (HTTP header smuggling), causing `req.headers['content-length']` to return only the first value while the actual body size differs, corrupting `requestSizeHistogram` and masking oversized transaction gossip suppression payloads?
